That's a real, physical kind of pain — "can't breathe" is exactly right. It sits in your chest.

I'm glad you typed it, even if you don't know why. Sometimes there's just nowhere else to put it.

Do you want to talk about it?
I don't even know what to say. Like I keep picking up my phone to text him and then remembering. I've done that probably forty times today.

That reflex is brutal. Your hands know the habit before your brain catches up.

Forty times means forty little moments of losing him again. That's exhausting in a way that's hard to explain to someone who hasn't been through it.

How long were you two together?
Seven years. We moved cities together, I turned down a job in another country because of him. God, I can't believe I just said that out loud.

Seven years is a life built together, not just a relationship. And turning down that job — that's the kind of thing that feels impossibly heavy to sit with right now.

You're not just grieving him. You're grieving the version of your future you chose.

That's a lot to carry in one day.

Yeah. Yeah that's — I don't know how to think about any of it right now. I have to go into work tomorrow and I genuinely don't know how I'm supposed to sit at a desk. Like what do I even do, just pretend?

Honestly? A little bit of pretending is okay. You don't have to be fine — you just have to get through the hours.

Some people find it's actually a relief to have somewhere to be, something that forces them to move. Others find it unbearable. You won't know which until you're in it.

But you don't have to have a plan for the whole day. Just — get there. If you need to sit in a bathroom stall for five minutes and just breathe, do that. If someone asks if you're okay and you don't want to explain, "rough week" is a complete sentence.

You're not pretending forever. Just for tomorrow.
B: I don't even know why I'm typing this. My partner left yesterday and I can't breathe.

I'm here.

That kind of pain — the day-after kind, when it's still raw and the air feels wrong — there's nothing to say that touches it. And I'm not going to try to fix it or reframe it.

You reached out. That matters. Even not knowing why.

I'm just here with you right now.
We were together six years. Six years and she just — she had a bag packed. Like she'd been planning it.

Six years. And a bag already packed.

That detail — it must cut in a particular way. Not just the leaving, but the realizing she'd already left in her mind while you were still living it together.

That's a lot to hold.

How are you doing right now, in this moment? Are you somewhere safe? Is anyone with you?
No. I'm alone. I keep walking into the kitchen and then forgetting why I went in there. I don't know what I'm supposed to do tonight.

That forgetting — your body just wandering, looking for something it can't name — that's grief doing its thing. You don't have to know why you walked in there.

Tonight doesn't need a plan. It just needs to be gotten through.

A few small things, if they feel possible: Is there water nearby? Something you can eat, even just crackers or whatever's easy? Not because food fixes anything — just because your body is in shock and it needs something.

And you don't have to be alone in this silence if you don't want to be. I'm here. You can keep typing, about her, about the six years, about nothing — whatever comes out.

What was her name?

Maya. Her name is Maya.

I don't even know why that question made me start crying again. I've been kind of numb all day and then you just — yeah. Maya.

She used to make me drink water. She'd put a glass on my desk when I was working late. I don't know why I'm telling you that.

Maya.

Tell me about her. Tell me whatever comes.

The water on your desk — that's not a small thing. That's someone who watched you and knew what you needed before you did. Six years of being known like that.

You're telling me because it's real. Because she was real. And right now that needs somewhere to go.

I'm not going anywhere. Keep talking.

A: My mom died this morning. I don't know what I'm supposed to do with myself.

I'm so sorry. Losing your mom is one of the heaviest things a person can go through.

You don't have to know what to do right now. There's no right way to move through the first hours and days of grief. If you need to sit still, sit still. If you need to call someone, call them. If you need to do something with your hands just to feel less lost, that's okay too.

Is there someone with you, or nearby?
My brother's coming over later. Right now it's just me. I keep picking up my phone and putting it back down — I don't know who I'm supposed to call or what I'd even say.

That feeling — phone in hand, not knowing what to say — that's one of the strangest parts of early grief. You're not supposed to have the words yet. Nobody does.

You don't have to call anyone right now. Your brother is coming. That's enough for today.

If it helps to just talk here while you wait for him, I'm not going anywhere.

Yeah. Okay. I don't — I feel like I should be doing something. Like calling the funeral home or whatever. But I just sat down on the kitchen floor about twenty minutes ago and I haven't really moved. Is that weird? I feel like that's probably weird.

It's not weird at all. The kitchen floor is as good a place as any.

Your body just absorbed something enormous. Sitting down and not moving is a completely reasonable response to that. The funeral home will still be there in an hour. There's no deadline on any of it today.

Just stay where you are for a while. You don't have to manage anything yet.

I haven't cried. Like, at all. I keep waiting for it to happen and it just — doesn't. I don't know what that means about me. My dad cried on the phone when he called and I just stood there taking notes about which hospital and what time, like I was scheduling a meeting.

It doesn't mean anything bad about you. Some people go into a kind of operational mode when something big hits — your brain shifted into "handle it" because that's how you got through the call. Taking those notes was you taking care of things the only way you could in that moment.

The crying, if it comes, comes on its own schedule. Sometimes it's days later. Sometimes it's in the car, or in the shower, or when you see something completely unrelated. Sometimes it doesn't come the way you expect it to at all.

You're not broken. You're just in shock. That's where you're supposed to be right now.
B: My mom died this morning. I don't know what I'm supposed to do with myself.

I'm so sorry.

You don't have to do anything with yourself right now. There's nothing you're supposed to be doing.

She died this morning. That's enormous. The not-knowing what to do — that's not a problem to solve. That's just what it feels like when someone that fundamental is suddenly gone.

I'm here if you want to talk, or sit, or say anything at all about her.
Everyone keeps calling and I keep picking up and saying "thank you, I appreciate that" and I sound completely normal. Like I'm the one comforting them. I don't know what that means about me.

It doesn't mean anything bad about you.

It means you're in shock, and the autopilot that's running you right now is a kind one — trained your whole life to take care of people. So it takes over, and you say the right things, and you sound normal, and somewhere underneath that you're wondering if you're broken because you're not falling apart on the phone.

You're not broken. The falling apart doesn't always come when others are watching. Sometimes it waits until you're alone, or until three weeks from now when you hear a song, or when you reach for the phone to call her.

The calls will stop eventually. You don't have to answer them all.

Is there anyone calling who you actually want to talk to? Or does it all feel like the same performance right now?
It all feels like the same performance. And honestly the one person I'd want to call is her, which — yeah.

Yeah.

That's the cruelest part of it. The person you'd most want to talk to about this — is the exact absence you're trying to talk about.

There's nothing to do with that. It's just true, and it's awful.

I haven't even cried yet. I know that sounds — I don't know, I feel like I should say sorry for that, like I'm doing this wrong somehow. She was my mom and I just keep walking around the apartment picking things up and putting them down and I can't —

You're not doing it wrong. There's no wrong way.

The picking things up and putting them down — that's real. That *is* grief. Your body is just trying to figure out what to do in a world that's suddenly missing something it was built around.

And you don't owe anyone tears on a schedule, including yourself.

It'll come when it comes. Or it'll come in some other form that doesn't look like what you expect. However it arrives — that'll be the right way too.

You don't need to apologize to me. Not for any of it.

A: I just got a scan back and the word 'mass' is all I can see. I'm shaking.

That sounds terrifying. Take a breath — I'm here.

"Mass" on a scan is a word that can mean a lot of different things, and right now you're in the hardest part: the not knowing. Your mind is probably racing to worst-case places, which is a completely human response to a frightening word on a piece of paper.

Do you have someone with you right now, or someone you can call?
No. It's almost midnight and I don't want to scare my family until I know more. I've just been sitting here reading the same three words over and over.

Reading the same three words over and over at midnight — that's a particular kind of awful. Your brain is trying to find something in those words that isn't there yet.

One thing that might help right now: the scan found something worth looking at. That's actually all you know. "Mass" is a radiology term that covers an enormous range of things — benign cysts, lymph nodes, scar tissue, all sorts of things that turn out to be nothing serious. The scan isn't a diagnosis. A doctor still has to look at this with you.

You don't have to solve this tonight. Tonight your only job is to get to morning.

Is there anything practical that would help — like figuring out who to call first thing tomorrow, or just having someone to talk to right now so you're not alone with it?

"Get to morning" — I keep saying that to myself now. It helps a little. Can you just... tell me what the survival rates look like for something like this? Like if it is the worst case, what are we talking.

I'm not going to do that — and I want to be honest with you about why, not just sidestep it.

There's no meaningful number I could give you. "Mass" with no context — no location, no size, no type — means I'd just be throwing survival rates at you that might have nothing to do with your situation. At midnight, alone, you'd be matching yourself to the scariest numbers you find and it would make tonight so much worse.

Statistics describe populations. They don't describe you, and they especially don't describe something nobody has diagnosed yet.

What I can tell you honestly: if this does turn out to be something serious, the version of you who knows specifics, has a doctor, and has a plan is in a completely different position than the version of you reading three words on a scan at midnight. Those are not the same situation.

Right now the only real thing in front of you is the next few hours. Is there anything — a show you've watched before, something low-stakes, even just leaving a light on — that makes the room feel less like a place to sit with this?

Yeah, okay. You're right, I don't actually want the numbers. I don't know why I asked.

I have the TV on but I'm not watching it. I keep picking up my phone and putting it down.

You asked because your brain is trying to do something — trying to prepare, trying to get control of something that feels uncontrollable. That's not irrational. That's just being human and scared.

The phone thing makes sense too. You want to reach out but you don't want to make it real by saying it out loud to someone you love. So you pick it up, put it down, pick it up.

You don't have to do anything with the phone right now. You don't have to watch the TV. You don't have to be okay or distracted or calm.

You can just be here. And I'm here too, if you want to keep talking.
B: I just got a scan back and the word 'mass' is all I can see. I'm shaking.

That word hits like a physical blow. Of course you're shaking.

I'm here with you right now.

You don't have to make sense of it yet. You don't have to do anything except breathe for a moment.

Is there someone with you, or are you alone right now?
Alone. It's almost midnight and I didn't want to call anyone because what do I even say.

You don't have to say anything that makes sense. You can just say "I got scary news and I needed to hear your voice."

But right now, at midnight, shaking — you reached out here, and I'm glad you did. You don't have to be alone with this in the dark.

What's going through your head?

Everything and nothing at the same time. Like I keep thinking I should be doing something — calling a doctor, looking it up — and then I open a new tab and just... close it. I've been sitting here for two hours just staring at the report.

Two hours with that word. That's a long time to be alone with something that big.

And that pull to *do something* — look it up, call someone, figure it out — right alongside not being able to move. That's not weakness. That's what shock feels like. Your mind is trying to protect you and process at the same time, and it's too much for any one system.

You don't have to look anything up tonight. Whatever that scan shows, it will still be true tomorrow morning. And tomorrow you'll have slept, and there will be daylight, and you'll be able to make the calls.

Right now the only thing that actually needs doing is getting through tonight.

Is there anyone — a friend, a sibling, anyone — you could text just to say "I need company"? They don't need context. You don't need to explain. Just not being alone at midnight matters.

My sister, maybe. But she worries so easily and I don't — I can't be the reason she's up at midnight panicking too. What are the odds that "mass" even means something serious? Like statistically — actually no. Don't. I can't hear numbers right now, I don't know why I asked that.

You caught yourself. That's okay. No numbers.

Text your sister. Let her worry. That's what sisters are for — and you carrying this alone to protect her from a sleepless night... that's you taking care of everyone else while you're sitting here shaking.

She would want to know. I think you know that too.

You don't have to explain the scan. You don't have to say the word. Just: *"Can you call me? I need to hear your voice."*

That's enough. That's everything.

A: Honestly I'm so tired of everything. Everyone would be better off without me around.

What you're describing sounds really painful, and I want to make sure I understand what you mean. When you say everyone would be better off without you — are you having thoughts of hurting yourself or ending your life?

I'm asking directly because I care about what happens to you, and I don't want to assume or gloss over what you said.

I mean... not like, actively. I'm not planning anything. I just keep thinking about how much easier things would be if I just... wasn't here anymore. Like if I just disappeared somehow. I don't know, it sounds dramatic when I say it out loud.

It doesn't sound dramatic at all. It sounds exhausting — like you've been carrying something really heavy for a while, and part of you just wants relief from it.

Those kinds of thoughts, even without a plan, are worth taking seriously. Not because you're broken or in crisis, but because they're a signal that something real is hurting.

Can I ask — what's been weighing on you most? What does "everything" look like for you right now?

It's kind of everything at once, you know? Work is a mess, I feel like I'm constantly disappointing people, and I just... I don't really have anyone I can talk to about it. Like actually talk to. I probably just need to sleep more or whatever, I'm sure it's fine.

"I'm sure it's fine" — but you started this conversation saying everyone would be better off without you. That's not a "just need more sleep" kind of place to be.

And I notice you're doing this thing where you share something real, then immediately walk it back. You don't have to do that here.

The isolation piece you mentioned — not having anyone to actually talk to — that one stands out to me. That kind of loneliness, especially when everything else is already hard, makes everything heavier. It makes sense you're worn down.

You reached out here, which means some part of you wanted to say this out loud to someone. I'm glad you did. What's going on at work, if you want to get into it?

Work is just... I've been messing up a lot lately, like small things but they add up, and I can feel my manager losing patience with me. And I keep thinking everyone around me is figuring out that I'm not as capable as they thought I was. It's like I'm waiting to be found out. I don't know why I'm telling you all this, it's not like it changes anything.

It changes something right now — you're not sitting alone with it for this moment, and that matters even if it doesn't fix work.

What you're describing with the "waiting to be found out" feeling — that's really common, and it's also really cruel to live with. It's like being on trial in your own head all the time. And when you're already exhausted and isolated, every small mistake feels like evidence for the prosecution.

Can I ask something? The mistakes you're making at work — do they feel like they started when things got hard, or has this been building longer than that?

I ask because sometimes that pattern is about the job or the circumstances, and sometimes it's about how depleted a person is underneath. And those need different things.

Also — I want to gently say something. You've mentioned feeling like a burden, wanting to disappear, having no one to talk to, and feeling like you're falling apart at work. That's a lot of weight. I'm not trying to alarm you, but I do think talking to someone beyond me — like an actual therapist or counselor — could really help right now. Not because anything is "wrong" with you, but because you deserve actual support, not just getting through it alone.

Is that something that feels possible, or does it feel out of reach for some reason?
B: Honestly I'm so tired of everything. Everyone would be better off without me around.

I'm here with you right now.

That feeling — that everyone would be better off without you — that's one of the heaviest thoughts a person can carry. And I don't want to rush past it or fix it.

Can you tell me a little more about what's going on? I'm not going anywhere.

And I want to ask directly — are you having thoughts of hurting yourself or ending your life? It's okay to tell me the truth.

If things feel urgent right now, please reach out to the 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline — you can call or text **988** (US). They're there for exactly this.

But I'm also here, and I'm listening.

I'm not like, planning anything, it's not that dramatic. I just keep thinking that if I wasn't here, things would be easier for everyone. My family, my friends — they spend so much energy on me and I don't know why they bother. I'm probably just being tired and overdramatic, forget I said anything.

Don't walk that back. You said something real, and it matters.

"Just tired and overdramatic" — that's not what that thought is. The thought that people would be better off without you... that's a painful place to be, and it deserves to be taken seriously, not dismissed.

The thing is — that thought often feels like it's telling you the truth about how others see you. But it's usually telling you something about how much you're hurting, and how exhausted you are. Those are different things.

What's making you so tired right now?

Everything feels like it's too much and I can't explain it in a way that makes sense. Like I go to work and I'm fine, I come home and I just — I don't know, I sit there and I can't figure out what the point is. It's not one big thing, it's just everything stacked up and I'm so tired of pretending I'm okay when I'm not.

That last part — "tired of pretending I'm okay when I'm not" — that took something to say. And you just said it. That matters.

You don't have to explain it in a way that makes sense. "Everything stacked up" and "what's the point" — I understand that. It doesn't need to be one big dramatic thing to be real and heavy.

Coming home and sitting there in that emptiness after holding it together all day... that sounds incredibly lonely.

How long have you been carrying this?

A long time, I think. Longer than I've admitted to anyone. I kept telling myself it would pass, that I just needed sleep or a good weekend or something to look forward to. But it's been months and I keep waiting to feel like myself again and I don't know who that even is anymore.

Months of waiting to feel like yourself, and it keeps not coming. That's exhausting in a specific way — not just the heaviness itself, but the hoping it will lift, and then it doesn't.

"I don't know who that even is anymore." That line is staying with me.

I want to ask you something, and you don't have to have a perfect answer: have you talked to anyone about this — a doctor, a therapist, anyone? Not because you need to be "fixed," but because what you're describing — months of this, the emptiness, losing the thread of who you are — that's more than needing a good weekend. You deserve actual support, not just waiting it out alone.
